text: Psychologist Phillippa Lally's 2010 study at University College London found that habit formation — defined as the point where a behavior becomes automatic — takes, on average, 66 days, with a range from 18 to 254 days depending on the behavior and the individual. The popular claim that habits form in 21 days traces to a misreading of plastic surgeon Maxwell Maltz's 1960 observation that patients took 'at least 21 days' to adjust to physical changes. Lally's data also revealed that missing a single day had no statistically significant effect on eventual automaticity — a finding that directly contradicts the all-or-nothing thinking that causes most habit-change attempts to collapse.
question: What did Lally's research find about missing a single day during habit formation?

options.text: Missing one day resets the automaticity timeline to zero
Missing one day extends formation time by an average of two weeks
Missing one day had no statistically significant effect on eventual automaticity
Missing one day was only inconsequential if it occurred after day 40
